meta.title: The Shape of the Many
meta.thesis: The wise crowd and the mob are the same people. One mechanism — local interaction producing group-level order — runs under different conditions, and a nameable condition flips the sign between collective wisdom and collective catastrophe.

books.title: The Shape Forms
books.gloss: The mechanism, its evolutionary roots, and the wise crowd.

chapters.title: Emergence
chapters.epigraph: A traffic jam can travel backward down a motorway while every car in it is moving forward — and no driver ordered the jam, chose it, or can find it in their own steering wheel.

chapters.title: Self-organisation
chapters.epigraph: Order can assemble itself with no one holding the blueprint — and the same process that finds the near-perfect answer also builds the fatal one.

chapters.title: Reynolds' boids
chapters.epigraph: Three rules, one neighbour at a time, and a shape no bird intended rises over the reeds at dusk.

chapters.title: Stigmergy
chapters.epigraph: Leave a mark, and the mark tells the next worker what to do — coordination without a word spoken, a plan drawn, or a single agent who knows the whole.

chapters.title: Positive & negative feedback loops
chapters.epigraph: Two loops run under everything a crowd does: one amplifies, one restrains. Their ratio decides whether a system settles or explodes — and the word "positive" is a warning, not a compliment.

chapters.title: Phase transitions & thresholds
chapters.epigraph: Order can hold, and hold, and hold — and then, at one more turn of a single dial, flip everything at once.

chapters.title: Granovetter's threshold model
chapters.epigraph: A riot and a quiet afternoon can be made of the exact same people; what differs is not who they are but the order in which they would move.

chapters.title: Complex adaptive systems
chapters.epigraph: Many parts, each following its own local rules, adapting to each other — and out of that ceaseless mutual adjustment comes a whole that no part intended and no one commands.

chapters.title: Hamilton's selfish herd
chapters.epigraph: The herd is not a brotherhood. It is a crowd of individuals each trying to put someone else between itself and the teeth.

chapters.title: The dilution effect
chapters.epigraph: The simplest reason to stand in a crowd is also the coldest: if the predator can only eat so many, every neighbour you gain is one more candidate to be eaten instead of you.

chapters.title: The predator confusion effect
chapters.epigraph: A hunter can starve inside a cloud of food, because a thousand identical targets are harder to catch than one.

chapters.title: Collective vigilance & the many-eyes effect
chapters.epigraph: Put enough heads together and the group sees a hawk before any single animal was even looking — but only if each still bothers to look.

chapters.title: The costs of group living
chapters.epigraph: Every benefit of the crowd arrives with an invoice, and the same density that buys safety also concentrates hunger, disease, and betrayal.

chapters.title: Reciprocal altruism
chapters.epigraph: I pay a cost to help you today because the relationship will pay me back tomorrow — and cooperation between strangers turns out to be a loan, not a gift.

chapters.title: Kin selection & inclusive fitness
chapters.epigraph: A gene for self-sacrifice can conquer a species, provided it spends its host on behalf of the copies of itself riding in the host's relatives.

chapters.title: Costly signalling & the handicap principle
chapters.epigraph: A signal is believed precisely because it is too expensive to fake — and the same waste that guarantees honesty can bankrupt those who must pay it.

chapters.title: Galton's ox
chapters.epigraph: Eight hundred people who each guessed wrong guessed the ox's weight together to within a pound — and the man who ran the experiment had expected them to prove the opposite.

chapters.title: Condorcet's Jury Theorem
chapters.epigraph: A crowd of the barely-competent can be nearly infallible — or reliably, catastrophically wrong. The same equation says both, and the sign is set by a single number.

chapters.title: The diversity prediction theorem
chapters.epigraph: A group's error is its members' average error minus their diversity — so difference is not decoration on the crowd's wisdom; it is a term that gets subtracted, exactly, from the crowd's mistake.

chapters.title: Surowiecki's four conditions
chapters.epigraph: A crowd is wise on four conditions and mad on the loss of any one of them; this chapter is the dial the whole book turns.

chapters.title: Independent-error cancellation
chapters.epigraph: Average enough honest mistakes and they annihilate each other; average enough shared mistakes and they add up. Same arithmetic, opposite fate — and only one word decides which.

chapters.title: The Delphi method
chapters.epigraph: The wise crowd, it turns out, can be built rather than found — if you engineer out the pressures that make experts in a room agree with the loudest person present.

chapters.title: Prediction markets
chapters.epigraph: A price is a crowd's probability, made honest by money — the same instrument that reads the future can also stop reading it and start reading itself.

chapters.title: Ensemble methods & random forests
chapters.epigraph: Machine learning rediscovered the wisdom of crowds — and then had to prove, in code, that the crowd is only wise if you force its members to disagree.

chapters.title: The collective intelligence factor "c"
chapters.epigraph: A group has an IQ, it is measurable, and it is set less by how clever its members are than by how they take turns.

books.title: The Shape Breaks
books.gloss: Cascades, panics, and conformity — the frontier of imposed silence.

chapters.title: Information cascades
chapters.epigraph: Watch enough people choose before you, and it becomes rational to stop thinking — at which point a thousand followers carry no more knowledge than the first two.

chapters.title: Availability cascades
chapters.epigraph: Say a thing often enough and it stops needing to be true; repetition is not evidence, but to a human mind it feels exactly like it.

chapters.title: Bank runs
chapters.epigraph: A sound bank can be destroyed by nothing more than the belief that it will be destroyed — the panic writes the reality it predicts.

chapters.title: Crowd crushes & stampede dynamics
chapters.epigraph: The crowd does not turn on itself; it simply runs out of room to breathe, and no one in it is deciding anything at all.

chapters.title: Manias & delusions
chapters.epigraph: Point the same crowd at the truth and it finds an ox's weight to the pound; let it price the future by watching itself and it will pay any sum for a bulb, a share, or a scapegoat.

chapters.title: Moral panics
chapters.epigraph: A society, frightened, turns and names one of its own the source of the fear — and the naming becomes the crisis it claimed to describe.

chapters.title: Deindividuation
chapters.epigraph: Put on the mask and the mob is supposed to swallow the self — but the newer evidence says the self does not vanish so much as change costume.

chapters.title: Emotional contagion
chapters.epigraph: Feeling travels from body to body below the threshold of choosing — and a crowd that catches one mood together has stopped being a crowd of independent minds.

chapters.title: Preference falsification
chapters.epigraph: A whole order can rest on a lie that no one believes and everyone tells — and it holds until, all at once, it doesn't.

chapters.title: Asch's conformity experiments
chapters.epigraph: Show a person an obvious truth, surround them with people calmly denying it, and a third of the time they will deny it too — unless a single other voice tells the truth first.

chapters.title: Sherif's autokinetic norms
chapters.epigraph: Put people in the dark with no fact to check against, and they will not wait in silence — they will build a truth together, and it will outlive every one of them.

chapters.title: Milgram's obedience studies
chapters.epigraph: The distance between an ordinary person and an instrument of harm is often just one voice in a lab coat saying *the experiment requires that you continue.*

chapters.title: Cialdini's six principles of influence
chapters.epigraph: Persuasion is not a dark art but a short list of switches — reliable, adaptive, and quietly waiting for anyone who knows where they are.

chapters.title: Social proof
chapters.epigraph: When we do not know what is true or right, we read the answer off the behaviour of the people around us — and that reading is wisdom or folly depending entirely on whether those people knew anything.

chapters.title: The bystander effect
chapters.epigraph: The more people who witness an emergency, the less likely any one of them is to help — because a duty divided by a crowd can shrink to nothing.

chapters.title: Normative versus informational influence
chapters.epigraph: There are two entirely different reasons to agree with a crowd — one changes your mind, the other only changes your mouth — and telling them apart is the key that unlocks everything else.

chapters.title: Pluralistic ignorance
chapters.epigraph: A room can hold, unanimously and in public, a conviction that almost every one of its members privately rejects — and each will point to the others as the reason it must be true.

chapters.title: Groupthink
chapters.epigraph: The most cohesive, harmonious, confident group in the room is often the one about to make the worst decision — because comfort has quietly killed the dissent that error-correction requires.

books.title: The Shape Flows
books.gloss: Networks and contagion: how the shape travels.

chapters.title: Small-world networks
chapters.epigraph: Your friends all know each other, and yet six handshakes reach a stranger on the far side of the planet — and the second fact is the one that makes a rumour a wildfire.

chapters.title: The strength of weak ties
chapters.epigraph: The news that changes your life rarely comes from your closest friends — they know what you already know; it comes from the acquaintance at the edge of your world, whose value is precisely that they are not close.

chapters.title: Scale-free networks & hubs
chapters.epigraph: In many of the networks that matter, a handful of nodes hold most of the connections — and whoever holds the hubs holds the flow.

chapters.title: Centrality — locating power in a network
chapters.epigraph: "Who matters here?" has at least four honest answers, and they do not always name the same person.

chapters.title: Homophily
chapters.epigraph: Birds of a feather flock together — and in flocking, they quietly strip their own networks of the one thing a crowd needs to be wise.

chapters.title: Structural holes & brokerage
chapters.epigraph: Between two worlds that never speak lies a gap, and whoever stands in it holds a small monopoly on the traffic — free to enrich both sides, or to keep them apart because the keeping pays.

chapters.title: Echo chambers & filter bubbles
chapters.epigraph: Remove every voice that might correct you, and the crowd around you stops being a check on your error and becomes a mirror of it.

chapters.title: Dunbar's number
chapters.epigraph: There may be a ceiling on how many people you can truly know — and above that line, a society has to be held together by something other than knowing.

chapters.title: Diffusion of innovations
chapters.epigraph: A new idea does not arrive everywhere at once; it seeps through a population along the lines of who trusts whom, and the shape it traces over time is always the same slow-then-sudden-then-slow curve.

chapters.title: Epidemics of ideas — the SIR model
chapters.epigraph: An idea spreads by the same arithmetic as a disease, and the arithmetic does not ask whether the idea is true.

chapters.title: Simple versus complex contagion
chapters.epigraph: Some things you catch from a single touch; others you adopt only after several people you trust have shown you it is safe — and the same network wiring that speeds the first can strangle the second.

chapters.title: Two-step flow & opinion leaders
chapters.epigraph: The message from the megaphone rarely reaches you straight; it reaches you through someone you already trust — which is why capturing a handful of the trusted moves the many without touching them at all.

chapters.title: Memetics
chapters.epigraph: An idea does not spread because it is true or good; it spreads because it is good at spreading — and nothing in the spreading cares which.

chapters.title: Virality & network thresholds
chapters.epigraph: A hit is not a better song sung louder; it is an ordinary thing that happened to land in a network primed to pass it on.

chapters.title: The first-follower dynamic
chapters.epigraph: A lone act of defiance is only madness until one other person joins it — the second is the one who makes the first a leader, and the crowd a possibility.

chapters.title: Tipping points
chapters.epigraph: Pressure accumulates invisibly, then a single increment crosses a line and the whole system flips — and the line has no opinion about whether what comes next is a liberation or a panic.

books.title: The Shape Holds Power
books.gloss: Markets, governance, and ideology — the climax.

chapters.title: Efficient markets versus behavioural finance
chapters.epigraph: A price is a crowd's whispered verdict on the future — and the whole question of this book is whether that verdict is the ox-guessers or the mob.

chapters.title: Keynes' beauty contest
chapters.epigraph: When the prize goes to whoever guesses the crowd's taste, nobody is left looking at the faces — only at each other.

chapters.title: Minsky's financial instability hypothesis
chapters.epigraph: The calm does not merely precede the storm — it builds it. Stability is the weather in which fragility is manufactured, and the longer it lasts the more it makes.

chapters.title: Institutional herding
chapters.epigraph: The safest place to be wrong is in a crowd of experts who are wrong the same way you are — which is precisely how a market of the wise becomes a mob.

chapters.title: Bubbles & crashes
chapters.epigraph: Every mania is the same manias wearing new clothes, and every crash is the crowd's price-finding machine caught running in reverse.

chapters.title: Reflexivity
chapters.epigraph: In some domains the crowd is not guessing at a fact that sits still while it guesses — it is helping to make the fact it thinks it is describing.

chapters.title: The tragedy of the commons — and Ostrom's rebuttal
chapters.epigraph: Put strangers on a shared pasture and it dies; put a community on it and it can feed the village for six hundred years — the difference is not the grass but whether the herders can talk.

chapters.title: Schelling's segregation model
chapters.epigraph: Nobody in the room wanted a wall, and yet the room built one — mild preferences, iterated, can construct an outcome that not a single person chose.

chapters.title: Honeybee democracy
chapters.epigraph: Several hundred bees, none in charge, choose the single best home out of a dozen — and get it right almost every time.

chapters.title: Ostrom's eight principles
chapters.epigraph: The tragedy of the commons is not a law of nature but a failure of design; give a community the right eight conditions and it will govern its shared world for centuries.

chapters.title: Consensus, majority, and quorum
chapters.epigraph: The rule you use to count the room decides who the room becomes; there is no neutral way to turn many minds into one decision.

chapters.title: Arrow's impossibility theorem
chapters.epigraph: There is no fair way to turn many rankings into one — not because we have not yet found the rule, but because a proof says the rule cannot exist.

chapters.title: Deliberative democracy and its failure modes
chapters.epigraph: Talking together can supply the one thing raw voting lacks — informed, reason-giving diversity — or destroy the one thing that made the crowd wise: the independence of the people in the room.

chapters.title: Liquid & delegated democracy
chapters.epigraph: A vote you can cast yourself, hand to someone you trust, or take back before breakfast — democracy with a dial between direct and representative, turned issue by issue.

chapters.title: Jury deliberation
chapters.epigraph: Twelve strangers are sealed in a room and told to find the truth together — and in nine cases out of ten, the room only ratifies the verdict it walked in believing.

chapters.title: Collective action & free-riding
chapters.epigraph: A hundred people who would each gain from a thing they must build together will often build nothing — because each of them is right, alone, to wait for the others.

chapters.title: Durkheim's collective effervescence
chapters.epigraph: When bodies gather and move as one, they generate a heat they cannot believe comes from themselves — so they call it God, or the nation, or the cause, and worship the thing their own assembly has made.

chapters.title: Le Bon and The Crowd
chapters.epigraph: The founding text of crowd psychology got the crowd exactly half right — and the missing half was so dangerous that dictators built their trade on it.

chapters.title: Freud on the group and the leader
chapters.epigraph: A crowd is not held together by contagion alone; it is held together by a love it has all placed in the same man, and having loved him together, its members become as one.

chapters.title: Canetti's Crowds and Power
chapters.epigraph: The crowd is not one thing to be judged but a family of forms to be described — and the same bodies that gather to hunt a victim can gather to overthrow their masters.

chapters.title: Cultural evolution and dual inheritance
chapters.epigraph: Humans inherit through two channels at once — genes and culture — and the second is an evolutionary system in its own right, obeying its own rules, capable of accumulating wisdom no mind could reach and error no mind would choose.

chapters.title: Ritual & behavioural synchrony
chapters.epigraph: Move together long enough and the boundary between you and the person beside you begins to thin — which is exactly why every power that ever wanted a unified body has put its people in step.

chapters.title: Propaganda & manufactured consent
chapters.epigraph: Propaganda is not the art of making people believe lies; it is the art of deciding what they will think about, and of arranging that the answer look like their own.

chapters.title: Shared fictions as coordination devices
chapters.epigraph: A banknote is a worthless scrap of cotton that moves armies, feeds cities, and topples governments — because enough of us agree, all at once, to act as if it were not.

chapters.title: Identity fusion & sacred values
chapters.epigraph: A person will not die for a payoff, but they will die for a group that has become the self and a value that will not be priced — and the same fusion makes both the hero and the killer.

chapters.title: Stratification as collective behaviour
chapters.epigraph: A caste is not a thing that exists; it is a thing that is done — re-done every morning by the millions it ranks, until the doing looks like nature.

books.title: The Shape Remade
books.gloss: Digital swarms, the toolkit, and epistemic humility.

chapters.title: Swarm intelligence & swarm robotics
chapters.epigraph: Take the rules an ant colony stumbled into over a hundred million years, write them into code and cheap machines, and you have built collective intelligence on purpose — with all of its promise and all of its menace intact.

chapters.title: Recommendation algorithms as herding engines
chapters.epigraph: A recommendation is a machine whispering what everyone like you already chose — and the whisper is what makes them choose it.

chapters.title: Outrage & virality on platforms
chapters.epigraph: A medium that sells attention will breed the emotion that seizes it — and the emotion that seizes it fastest is anger at the people we already hate.

chapters.title: Bots, astroturfing & manufactured consensus
chapters.epigraph: If you cannot win the crowd, you can forge one — and a forged crowd, believed, recruits the real one.

chapters.title: Online mobs & pile-ons
chapters.epigraph: A thousand people each throwing a single stone still leave a body — and no one who threw feels they killed anyone.

chapters.title: Wikipedia & open source as stigmergy
chapters.epigraph: Thousands of strangers who never speak build an encyclopedia and an operating system by leaving marks in a shared thing — the same trace-following that a feed uses to herd you, aimed instead at truth.

chapters.title: Superforecasting & forecasting tournaments
chapters.epigraph: Take ordinary people, protect their independence, train them to think in probabilities, aggregate their guesses with a little arithmetic — and they will out-predict the experts, even the ones holding the secrets.

chapters.title: Crowdsourcing & citizen science
chapters.epigraph: Give a problem too large for any expert to too many strangers, and the answer walks in through a million doors at once — but so does the question of who is paying whom.

chapters.title: Agent-based modelling
chapters.epigraph: Write down a few simple rules for a few simple people, set them loose on a grid, and watch a society you never designed climb out of the arithmetic.

chapters.title: Game theory & the prisoner's dilemma
chapters.epigraph: Two people, each doing the smartest thing they can, walk together into the outcome both least wanted — and the mathematics says they were right to.

chapters.title: The evolution of cooperation
chapters.epigraph: The prisoner's dilemma says the selfish defect into ruin — until the game repeats, at which point the very same selfish agents build stable cooperation, and no one had to become good.

chapters.title: Cellular automata
chapters.epigraph: A grid of cells, each obeying the same trivial rule about its neighbours, will build gliders, clocks, and universal computers — proof that complexity needs no complex cause.

chapters.title: Power laws & heavy tails
chapters.epigraph: Draw the distribution of almost anything a crowd does together and you get the same lopsided silhouette — a few giants, a long train of the small, and no such thing as a typical size.

chapters.title: The statistical physics of collective motion
chapters.epigraph: A flock, a swarm, a fleeing crowd: strip away the biology and the same short equation governs when order appears — and it does not care what the moving things are.

chapters.title: The epistemics of the field
chapters.epigraph: A book about how crowds fool themselves must, at the end, ask whether the crowd that produced its evidence was fooling itself too — and about whom, exactly, all of it was measured.
